refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' clause which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague; 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is more precise and solution-neutral. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the acceptance criterion context without prescribing how to achieve it.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate across the communication channel. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and packet loss statistics.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE teleoperation communication channel shall reliably transmit operator commands to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE teleoperation communication channel shall reliably transmit equipment state and sensor feedback to the operator.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency anomalies) to the operator and safety systems.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.
DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to customer network conditions, to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP4: Implement a quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation and traffic prioritization layer that reserves bandwidth for critical control signals and adapts payload compression dynamically.

DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.

potential_sources: Heartbeat monitoring is loosely coupled to retransmission logic via shared network state (loss rate estimation)—acceptable if state is read-only to heartbeat module.
SACK mechanism depends on frame classification (critical vs. non-critical), which must be defined consistently across command/feedback layers—requires clear interface contract.
Adaptive timeout calculation couples to latency measurement; ensure measurement module is independent and provides clean API to retransmission scheduler.
DP5 assumes customer-defined thresholds are externalized as configuration parameters; tight coupling to acceptance criteria is acceptable if thresholds are injected at initialization.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids hard dependencies on specific network topology (dual-channel not required) and does not mandate redundant hardware. Modularity is preserved if heartbeat, SACK, and retransmission are implemented as separate, event-driven components sharing a common network state interface. Risk: if timeout adaptation is too aggressive, may oscillate; mitigation via hysteresis and customer tuning.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network conditions are stable or change slowly enough for adaptive algorithms to converge within acceptable time windows (e.g., seconds to minutes). RISK: Rapid network degradation (e.g., sudden congestion) may cause transient control loss before adaptation completes. MITIGATION: Define fallback behavior (e.g., safe hold state) and require customer to specify expected network stability profile.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands and feedback frames can be classified into 'critical' (e.g., emergency stop, position updates) and 'non-critical' (e.g., diagnostic telemetry) categories. RISK: Misclassification may lead to loss of essential control signals. MITIGATION: Require explicit customer definition of frame priority and validate during acceptance testing.

ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is the dominant failure mode; latency and jitter are within acceptable bounds. RISK: If latency is high or variable, retransmission timeouts may be difficult to tune, leading to either excessive retransmission or missed recovery. MITIGATION: Measure and report latency percentiles; require customer to confirm latency SLA alongside loss SLA.

ASSUMPTION: Heartbeat and SACK mechanisms add acceptable overhead (e.g., <5% of bandwidth). RISK: On severely bandwidth-constrained links, overhead may itself trigger congestion. MITIGATION: Make heartbeat interval and SACK granularity configurable; provide guidance on overhead vs. reliability trade-off.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive timeout calculation uses standard algorithms (e.g., TCP-like RTT estimation). RISK: Pathological network conditions (e.g., bimodal latency distribution) may confuse estimator. MITIGATION: Implement robust estimators (e.g., median-based) and expose tuning parameters to customer.

ASSUMPTION: DP5 is implemented in software at the application or middleware layer. RISK: If network stack is not under system control (e.g., managed by third-party router), adaptive mechanisms may be ineffective. MITIGATION: Clarify network architecture assumptions in customer requirements; specify which network layers are in scope.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical control and feedback frames are delivered with ≤ [customer-defined]% packet loss under [customer-defined network conditions: bandwidth, latency percentiles, loss profile, stability assumptions]. Non-critical telemetry frames may be subject to higher loss tolerance. The solution shall adapt its redundancy and retransmission strategy within [adaptation time window, e.g., 10–30 seconds] to changing network conditions and shall provide a safe fallback behavior (e.g., hold last command) if adaptation cannot meet the target within [timeout threshold]. Acceptance testing shall validate loss rate, latency, and adaptation behavior under simulated network profiles provided by the customer.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote control communication protocols, including heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment of critical frames, adaptive timeout-driven retransmission, and latency-reliability trade-off logic for teleoperated vehicle commands and feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits heartbeat packets, SACK frames, and retransmitted critical commands; receives acknowledgments and network status feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control inputs that require reliable delivery with adaptive timeout tuning.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports heartbeat health status, retransmission statistics, and adaptive timeout parameters for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals critical communication failures (e.g., heartbeat loss, excessive retransmissions) that may trigger safety interventions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: heartbeat, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are core teleoperation protocol concerns. Latency-reliability trade-off is intrinsic to remote control. Minor diffusion: timeout parameters may be tuned by Safety Services for critical maneuvers.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns actuators and sensors, not communication protocols. DP5 is fundamentally a network-layer concern, not vehicle dynamics. High diffusion: protocol logic would leak into vehicle control stack.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety policies and fault detection, not communication protocol implementation. DP5 is a transport-layer mechanism, not a safety policy. Moderate diffusion: Safety Services would need to query protocol state and override timeouts, creating tight coupling.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. DP5 could be housed here, but it is too specific and would be better owned by a subsystem. High diffusion: protocol details would not be encapsulated, making reuse and testing difficult.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment, and adaptive retransmission are quintessential remote control communication concerns. These mechanisms directly address the latency-reliability trade-off inherent in teleoperated systems. The module boundary cleanly separates protocol logic (owned by Teleoperation) from safety policy (owned by Safety Services) and vehicle dynamics (owned by Vehicle), minimizing coupling and maximizing cohesion.
containment_risks: Timeout adaptation logic may need to be influenced by Safety Services during high-risk maneuvers, requiring a well-defined interface for timeout override requests.
Heartbeat and SACK frame formats must be compatible with the Network Link interface; any changes to network encoding could require protocol rework.
Retransmission statistics exported via Telemetry must be standardized to avoid tight coupling with monitoring systems.

If Vehicle module requires real-time acknowledgment of command receipt (e.g., for safety-critical steering), bidirectional coupling between Teleoperation and Vehicle could increase; this should be managed via explicit SafetyAlert signaling rather than direct protocol queries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module owns heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission—all directly measurable via packet loss metrics (≤1% target) and retransmission statistics exported via Telemetry interface. Acceptance criteria can be validated through network simulation and field testing under agreed conditions. SafetyAlert interface provides observable signal for critical communication failures.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR requirement maps to DP5 (hybrid approach) and is allocated to Teleoperation module (92% containment score). Ownership boundary is well-defined: Teleoperation owns protocol logic; Safety Services owns policy override; Vehicle owns actuator/sensor dynamics. Interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are explicit and directional.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses FR requirement. Hybrid approach (heartbeat + SACK + adaptive timeout-driven retransmission) is a sound design solution for maintaining communication integrity under packet loss. The mechanism balances latency and reliability as required. DP is sufficiently specific to guide implementation and measurable against acceptance criteria (packet loss ≤1%).

diffusion_risks: Timeout adaptation logic: Safety Services may need to override adaptive timeouts during high-risk maneuvers (e.g., critical steering commands). Risk: tight coupling if Safety Services directly modifies Teleoperation state. Mitigation: define explicit timeout-override request interface (SafetyAlert direction should be bidirectional or supplemented with a 'SafetyPolicy' input interface).

Heartbeat/SACK frame format compatibility: Network Link interface changes (encoding, packet structure) could require protocol rework. Risk: DP5 assumes stable network layer contract. Mitigation: document network encoding contract and version control frame formats.

Retransmission statistics export: Telemetry interface exports adaptive timeout parameters and retransmission counts. Risk: if monitoring systems tightly couple to specific metric formats, changes to DP5 (e.g., new timeout algorithm) could break downstream consumers. Mitigation: standardize Telemetry schema and version it.

Vehicle-Teleoperation bidirectional coupling: If Vehicle module requires real-time acknowledgment of command receipt for safety-critical steering, direct protocol queries between modules could increase coupling. Risk: violates clean separation of concerns. Mitigation: route all safety-critical feedback through SafetyAlert interface; avoid direct Vehicle→Teleoperation queries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (92% score). Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5. Heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are quintessential remote control communication concerns, not vehicle dynamics or safety policy. Module boundary cleanly separates protocol logic from safety policy and vehicle control. Minor diffusion (8%) is acceptable and managed via well-defined external interfaces. No evidence of DP5 logic scattered across multiple modules.

recommended_revisions: Clarify SafetyAlert interface directionality: Currently marked 'out' only. If Safety Services must override timeouts during critical maneuvers, add a bidirectional 'SafetyPolicy' input interface to Teleoperation, or explicitly document that timeout overrides are requested via SafetyAlert and acknowledged via Telemetry.

Define Network Link contract: Document heartbeat packet format, SACK frame structure, and retransmission protocol version. Establish change control to prevent unplanned diffusion when network encoding evolves.

Standardize Telemetry schema: Specify the structure and versioning of retransmission statistics and adaptive timeout parameters exported via Telemetry. This prevents tight coupling with downstream monitoring systems.

Add acceptance test criteria for timeout adaptation: Beyond packet loss ≤1%, define measurable criteria for adaptive timeout behavior (e.g., 'timeout shall adjust within X ms of network condition change' or 'retransmission latency shall not exceed Y ms under Z% packet loss'). This ensures DP5 implementation is verifiable.

Document Vehicle-Teleoperation interaction boundary: Explicitly state that Vehicle module shall not directly query Teleoperation protocol state. All safety-critical feedback must flow through SafetyAlert interface to maintain clean separation of concerns.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the current FR is well-formed and DP5 is a sound design solution with excellent module containment (92%). However, the gate results surface five concrete architectural and interface clarifications that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. These are not showstoppers but are critical for safety-critical systems: (1) SafetyAlert directionality ambiguity could lead to unsafe timeout override behavior if not clarified; (2) Network Link contract instability poses diffusion risk; (3) Telemetry schema versioning prevents downstream coupling; (4) Acceptance test criteria for adaptive timeout behavior are underspecified; (5) Vehicle-Teleoperation interaction boundary needs explicit documentation to prevent unsafe direct queries. Addressing these revisions at the current level ensures the FR and DP are unambiguous before decomposing into sub-FRs (e.g., heartbeat monitoring, SACK handling, adaptive retransmission logic). This is a high-safety-criticality system; clarity now prevents rework downstream.

revise.why: Revise at FR, DP, and IM levels concurrently: (1) FR: Clarify that 'maintaining communication integrity under packet loss' includes explicit safety-critical timeout override requirements—add acceptance criterion 'Safety Services shall be able to override adaptive timeouts during high-risk maneuvers with latency ≤X ms'. (2) DP5: Extend hybrid approach specification to include bidirectional SafetyPolicy input interface (or document SafetyAlert as bidirectional) and define timeout override request/acknowledgment protocol. Add measurable criteria for adaptive timeout adjustment speed (e.g., 'timeout shall converge to network conditions within 500 ms'). (3) IM: Explicitly document Teleoperation module interfaces as: [IN] Network Link (heartbeat/SACK frames), [IN] Tasking (command queue), [IN] SafetyPolicy (timeout override requests), [OUT] SafetyAlert (critical failures), [OUT] Telemetry (retransmission stats, timeout parameters). Add version control requirement for Network Link frame format and Telemetry schema. Document Vehicle module constraint: 'Vehicle shall not directly query Teleoperation protocol state; all safety-critical feedback routed via SafetyAlert interface only.'

assumptions: Safety Services has authority to override adaptive timeouts during high-risk maneuvers and can do so with acceptable latency.
Network Link interface (heartbeat/SACK frame format) is stable or has a documented change control process.
Telemetry schema for retransmission statistics and timeout parameters can be versioned independently of DP5 implementation changes.
Vehicle module does not require real-time direct protocol state queries; SafetyAlert interface provides sufficient feedback for safety-critical steering decisions.
Adaptive timeout algorithm can be tuned to converge to network conditions within acceptable time bounds (assumed ≤500 ms based on typical teleoperation latency budgets).
open_risks: SafetyAlert directionality: If currently unidirectional (Teleoperation→Safety Services), timeout override requests from Safety Services may not reach Teleoperation in time during critical maneuvers. Mitigation: confirm bidirectional capability or add explicit SafetyPolicy input interface.

Network Link contract drift: If Network Link encoding evolves (e.g., new packet types, compression schemes), DP5 heartbeat/SACK logic may require rework. Mitigation: establish version control and backward compatibility requirements.

Telemetry coupling: Downstream monitoring systems may tightly couple to specific retransmission metric formats. If DP5 adaptive algorithm changes, metrics may become meaningless. Mitigation: standardize schema and version it; document metric semantics.

Timeout override latency: If Safety Services override requests experience network delays or processing latency >X ms, critical steering commands may execute with stale timeout values. Mitigation: measure end-to-end override latency in acceptance testing.

Vehicle-Teleoperation coupling: If Vehicle module directly queries Teleoperation protocol state (e.g., 'is heartbeat alive?'), changes to DP5 heartbeat logic could break Vehicle safety logic. Mitigation: enforce interface boundary via code review and architectural testing.
evidence_needed: Documented SafetyAlert/SafetyPolicy interface specification with directionality, message types, and latency requirements for timeout override requests.
Network Link frame format specification (heartbeat packet structure, SACK encoding, protocol version) with change control process.
Telemetry schema definition for retransmission statistics and adaptive timeout parameters, including versioning strategy.

Acceptance test plan for adaptive timeout behavior: (a) timeout adjustment latency under network condition changes, (b) timeout override request latency from Safety Services, (c) retransmission latency under various packet loss rates (0–10%), (d) heartbeat detection latency (time to detect link failure).
Vehicle-Teleoperation interaction boundary specification: explicit list of allowed and forbidden queries/state accesses; confirmation that all safety-critical feedback flows via SafetyAlert.
Network simulation or field test results validating DP5 under target packet loss conditions (≤1% nominal, up to 5% transient) with measured retransmission statistics and timeout adaptation behavior.
